name: Aria Automation: Cloud Account Inventory Collection Failing — Insufficient Permissions
description: An Aria Automation vCenter cloud account passes the connection test but data collection fails, leaving the inventory empty. The service account has a role at the datacenter level but is missing the required permission at the vCenter root object.
customerScenario: Our Aria Automation cloud account status shows 'Data Collection Failed'. When I test the connection it says success, but the inventory is completely empty — no VMs, no networks, no datastores. The account was working fine last week. Our vCenter administrator did a quarterly permission review and adjusted some service account roles. I can't tell what changed.
customerFollowUp: The vCenter admin says they tightened permissions and moved the Aria Automation service account role to apply only at the datacenter level instead of the root vCenter object. They thought it would be more secure. The connection test still passes because basic authentication works, but something about the inventory scan is broken now.
fixSteps: Log in to vCenter as an administrator
Navigate to the top-level vCenter Server object in the inventory (not a datacenter — the root vCenter node)
Check whether the Aria Automation service account has a role assigned at the root level
If the account only appears under a datacenter and not the root vCenter object, that is the problem
Click Add Permission at the root vCenter object level
Assign the minimum required role to the service account — either Read-Only or a custom 'Aria Automation' role with inventory browsing rights
Check 'Propagate to children' so the permission applies to all objects in the inventory
In Aria Automation, navigate to Infrastructure > Connections > Cloud Accounts and open the vCenter account
Trigger a manual data collection — inventory should now populate with VMs, networks, and datastores
hints: The connection test only checks that the service account can authenticate — it does not verify inventory access. A successful connection test with empty inventory almost always means the account can log in but lacks the permissions to read the vCenter object tree. Where in vCenter would you check what permissions a specific account has?

Aria Automation needs to discover the entire vCenter inventory — VMs, hosts, clusters, networks, datastores. For that discovery to work, the service account must have at minimum Read-Only permissions at the root vCenter object level (not just a datacenter), with 'Propagate to children' enabled. Check the vCenter root object's Permissions tab, not the datacenter.

The service account's role was moved from the root vCenter object to just the datacenter level. The root vCenter object is a separate permission boundary — permissions assigned only at the datacenter do not grant access to the top-level vCenter constructs Aria Automation needs to enumerate during initial discovery.

Go to the vCenter root node in the vSphere Client inventory tree. Click the Permissions tab and check if the Aria Automation service account appears there. If it only has permissions at the datacenter level, add it at the root vCenter object level with Read-Only (or your custom Aria Automation role) and 'Propagate to children'.

Fix: In vSphere Client, select the root vCenter Server object > Permissions tab > Add > assign the Aria Automation service account the Read-Only role (or custom role) > check Propagate to children > OK. Then in Aria Automation, open the cloud account and trigger data collection. The inventory will populate once the account can traverse the full vCenter object hierarchy.
learningObjectives: Understand that a successful Aria Automation connection test does not guarantee inventory collection will work
Identify that Aria Automation requires permissions at the root vCenter object level, not just at a datacenter
Assign a role to a service account at the vCenter root with propagation to restore inventory collection
Trigger a manual data collection in Aria Automation to verify the fix
author: vSphere Lab Wizard starter library
